name: Multiple Sclerosis - DMT-Only Patients (No Diagnosis)
description: Tricky test case: finds patients on MS DMTs who have NO formal multiple sclerosis diagnosis on their problem list (Path C). Unlike GERD or RA, MS DMTs are highly specific — very few non-MS patients receive natalizumab, ocrelizumab, dimethyl fumarate, or fingolimod. The few exceptions: ocrelizumab (rituximab successor) is used off-label for severe RA and other autoimmune conditions; alemtuzumab is also approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. The Synthea module weights this path lightly (~10%) to reflect this clinical reality. This test case is the INVERSE of GERD's Path C — useful for evaluating whether the LLM understands that medication specificity varies dramatically by drug class.
prompt: I want to find patients in our system who are taking multiple sclerosis disease-modifying therapy but don't have a formal MS diagnosis on their problem list. These are uncommon — MS DMTs are very specific to MS — but include cases where the diagnosis was made elsewhere and not yet imported, or where alemtuzumab is prescribed for chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

prompts.naive: Find patients on MS DMTs without a multiple sclerosis diagnosis.
prompts.expert: I need to identify the rare MS-DMT-without-MS-dx population. Two-step approach: first find patients with MedicationRequest for any MS DMT (RxNorm 354770 natalizumab, 117055 alemtuzumab, 1876366 ocrelizumab, 1373478 dimethyl fumarate, 1012896 Gilenya/fingolimod), then exclude patients with a Condition for MS (24700007, 426373005, 425500002, 428700003, 192927008). The remaining group is small — typically alemtuzumab for CLL or recently-diagnosed MS not yet documented in the problem list.

metadata.algorithm_path_description: Path C 'tricky' patients: have MS DMT but NO MS Condition resource. The Synthea module weights this path lightly (~10%) — unlike GERD's PPI Path C, MS DMTs are highly specific and Path C is small.
